Findings: Nature of Statutory Criteria/Case Summary: Patient requested reimbursement for RTC services (ASAM 3.5).  Per ASAM criteria, this patient doesn’t meet Level 3.5 criteria for RTC services for the time period under review. The ASAM criteria focus on six dimensions to determine the appropriate level of care:For dimension 1 (intoxication & associated risk, withdrawal & associated risks, & addiction medication needs), patient meets ASAM 3.5 requirements: patient’s intoxication & associated risk, withdrawal & associated risks, & addiction medication needs should be discussed & scored. Patient was a daily drinker but hadn’t used alcohol, Valium, or cocaine for 10 days prior to admission, & last experienced withdrawal symptoms two months prior to the date of denial. Patient didn’t require a medical detoxification & his vital signs have been within normal limits. For dimension 2 (biomedical conditions including physical health concerns & pregnancy-related concerns), patient meets ASAM 3.5 requirements: patient’s biomedical conditions should be discussed & scored. Patient had a history of diabetes mellitus, obesity, hyperlipidemia, & allergic rhinitis & reportedly overdosed on alcohol & ketamine about one month prior to admission. During his stay at the RTC, patient didn’t experience any acute medical problems & reported insomnia but declined sleep medications due to his work as a firefighter. For dimension 3 (psychiatric & cognitive conditions & history including active psychiatric symptoms & persistent disability), patient doesn’t meet ASAM 3.5 requirements: patient’s psychiatric & cognitive conditions & history, including active psychiatric symptoms & persistent disability should be discussed & scored. Patient is experiencing psychiatric signs &/or symptoms at a level of acuity & complexity that interferes with addiction treatment & recovery & patient requires frequent (daily or near-daily) psychiatric management &/or nursing care to achieve sufficient stability to enable dictation in addiction treatment & patient has adequate impulse control to resist acting upon any thoughts of harm to self or others (if present) with daily or near-daily clinical support & patient cannot reliably access external psychiatric care to adequately address their mental health needs, &/or patient is assessed as requiring integrated skilled mental health interventions to permit participation in addiction treatment or patient meets Active Psychiatric Symptoms criteria for treatment at Level 1.7 COE & patient’s psychiatric signs &/or symptoms are likely to be exacerbated by intoxication, withdrawal, &/or comorbid Dimension 2 concerns & require daily or near-daily psychiatric management nursing care. For dimension 4 (substance-use-related risks including the likelihood of engaging in risky substance use & the likelihood of engaging in risky substance use disorder-related behaviors), patient meets ASAM 3.5 requirements: patient’s substance-use-related risks, including the likelihood of engaging in risky substance use & the likelihood of engaging in risky substance use disorder-related behaviors should be discussed & scored. Patient has a high likelihood of engaging in substance use with a significant risk of serious harm or destabilizing loss & patient requires 24-hour clinical support & supervision to prevent substance use while developing recovery-sustaining skills or patient otherwise meets the Likelihood of Engaging in Risky Substance Use criteria for treatment at Level 3.1 but, due to interactions with Dimensions 2 &/or 3 requires 24-hour supervision &/or more intensive clinical services for safety for stability or patient otherwise meets Likelihood of Engaging in Risky Substance Use criteria for treatment at Level 2.5 but, due to interactions with Dimensions 3 &/or 5, requires 24-hour support & supervision to promote safety & stability &/or patient requires residential support to enable effective participation in care & is assessed as unable to safely &/or effectively engage in outpatient care with recovery residence support. For dimension 5 (recovery environment interactions including the ability to function effectively in the current environment, safety in the current environment, & support in the current environment), patient doesn’t meet ASAM 3.5 requirements: patient’s recovery environment interactions, including the ability to function effectively in the current environment, safety in the current environment, & support in the current environment should be discussed & scored. Patient has moderately severe functional impairment in life activities &/or social relationships & to further develop the basic interpersonal skills &/or skills of independent living necessary to support sustained recovery, patient requires 24-hour structure & support with the therapeutic milieu & opportunities to practice learned skills in the community on a limited basis or patient otherwise meets Ability to Function Effectively in Current Environment criteria for treatment at Level 2.1 but lacks a safe & sufficiently supportive after-hours environment or requires an after-hours therapeutic milieu to effectively address functional impairment & patient is unable to effectively participate in the rules-based milieu of a recovery residence or recovery residence milieu is unlikely to provide sufficient support OR patient otherwise meets Ability to Function Effectively in Current Environment criteria for treatment at Level 2.1 but is assessed as unlikely to reliably attend outpatient services. For dimension 6 (patient-centered considerations including barriers to care, patient preferences, & the need for motivational enhancement): patient’s barriers to care, patient preferences, & need for motivational enhancement should be considered. Patient’s barriers to care included a lack of a stable living situation, a therapist to process his trauma, an outpatient addiction program, a transfer of probation to his current state, a new physician, a safe place to stay, & a job. Patient requested one year of treatment. Patient presented to treatment voluntarily & he was cooperative with all treatments at the RTC. Patient realized that he needed to get sober in order to live & needed to find new employment. Using the ASAM Level 3.5 criteria, patient doesn’t meet the criteria for medical necessity.Final Result: The reviewer determined that the services at issue were not medically necessary for the treatment of patient’s medical condition. Therefore, the Health Plan’s denial should be upheld. Findings: Nature of Statutory Criteria/Case Summary: The patient has requested reimbursement for SNF services. Admission to a SNF is appropriate for patients with conditions that require observation, evaluation of treatment plans, and updating of orders by a physician along with constantly available skilled nursing services. Skilled nursing services may include wound management, tracheostomy care, bowel and bladder training, tube feeding, and the administration of intravenous (IV) medications or medications that cannot be self-administered safely. Skilled nursing observation may include regular monitoring of vital signs, skin in the setting of wounds, and intake and output. Patients typically also benefit from skilled therapy services such as physical, occupational, and speech therapies due to physical limitations, a decline in function, or impaired cognition and communication. In this case, the patient is a 55-year-old female who was admitted for hospitalization due progressive weakness and numbness of the lower extremities. Her diagnoses included alcohol use disorder and electrolyte imbalance. The patient was nonambulatory. The patient was discharged to a SNF. Notes reported that the patient’s weakness may be due to Guillain-Barre Syndrome or toxic neuropathy due to a history of alcohol use. Upon admission to the SNF, the patient required minimal assistance for bathing, lower body dressing, and toileting, moderate assistance with bed mobility, and maximum assistance with transfers. Notes report that the patient currently requires supervision for bed mobility and contact-guard assistance for transfers. She is ambulating with a walker with minimal assistance. She requires stand-by assistance for eating and upper extremity bathing, and minimal assistance for toileting, lower body dressing, and bathing. The records note a target discharge date from the SNF. In this case, the patient’s conditions did not require the level of observation, evaluation, and treatment provided by a physician in the SNF setting. The patient was medically stable and tolerating a normal diet and oral medications. She continues to have lower extremity weakness and impaired mobility and function, but her neurological status is stable. She does not require skilled nursing services, including wound management, administration of IV medication, bowel or bladder training, or tube feeding. While the patient may continue to benefit from therapy or assistance, given that she does not require the intensity of therapy or the skilled services provided in the SNF setting, the services at issue were not medically indicated. Therefore, SNF services were not medically necessary for the treatment of this patient. Final Result: The reviewer determined that the services at issue were not medically necessary for treatment of the patient’s medical condition. Therefore, the Health Plan’s denial should be upheld. Credentials/Qualifications: The reviewer is board certified in physical medicine and rehabilitation and is actively practicing. The reviewer is an expert in the treatment of the enrollee’s medical condition and knowledgeable about the proposed treatment through recent or current actual clinical experience treating those with the same or a similar medical condition.

Findings: Findings:  The physician reviewer found that Nature of Statutory Criteria/Case Summary: The patient has requested authorization and coverage for right shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with contrast. Researchers explain, “Shoulder pain is one of the most common musculoskeletal complaints. Studies have shown an annual incidence of 14.7 per 1000 patients and a lifetime prevalence up to 70%. Shoulder pain affects work productivity, activities of daily life, and quality of life. Internal derangements of the shoulder including diseases of the muscles and tendons, joints, and labroligamentous and periarticular structures are common causes of shoulder pain. Diagnostic imaging has become increasingly important for the evaluation of these structures. MRI is a crucial diagnostic tool with its multiplanar imaging, high resolution, and lack of ionising radiation. Magnetic resonance arthrography is a more sensitive tool for the evaluation of the labroligamentous structures, joint capsule and cartilage, and additional signs of rotator cuff tears. Already the preferred imaging modality for evaluating many internal derangements of the shoulder, advances in MRI have provided additional potential benefits for evaluating shoulder pathology.” Researchers report that shoulder pain is a very common condition and can be debilitating. The authors note that several conservative therapies exist, such as exercise, physical therapy, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and ultrasound-guided procedures such as joint or bursal corticosteroid injections, fluid aspirations, and nerve blocks can be used. The authors further explain that MRI and ultrasound are the imaging modalities of choice for the evaluation of the rotator cuff tendons, while computed tomography (CT) and MRI are useful for evaluating the shoulder muscle bulk. Regarding the routine use of MRI in patients with clinically diagnosed frozen shoulder, researchers note, “Although additional pathologies were identified in 22% of patients, with 7.4% showing a full-thickness tear, a change in treatment plan due to the MRI findings was observed in only 2.7% of the patients. Given these numbers 37 MRIs are needed to identify one patient with frozen shoulder requiring surgery for the additional MRI findings. Therefore, routine use of shoulder MRI scans in patients with frozen shoulder but without suspicion of an additional pathology may not be indicated.” While the use of magnetic resonance arthrogram may be supported in the evaluation for the working diagnosis in this case, suspicion of a labral tear, the submitted records do not document the trial and failure of recent, reasonable, comprehensive, less invasive conservative care measures. Physical therapy notes submitted for review were four years ago. Shoulder pain is one of the most common musculoskeletal complaints and the use of advanced imaging, including magnetic resonance arthrogram, is not supported in peer-reviewed literature without failure of conservative measures and/or significant, worsening symptoms. The records document that the patient has full motion and strength. Given the limited published, large-scale, long-term peer-reviewed literature of the requested service as an effective and/or safe treatment for the noted pathology, the requested service is not medically indicated. Therefore, right shoulder MRI with contrast is not medically necessary for the evaluation of this patient.Final Result: The reviewer determined that the requested service is not medically necessary for evaluation of the patient’s medical condition. Therefore, the Health Plan’s denial should be upheld.Credentials/Qualifications: The reviewer is board certified in orthopedic surgery and is actively practicing. The reviewer is an expert in the treatment of the enrollee’s medical condition and knowledgeable about the proposed treatment through recent or current actual clinical experience treating those with the same or a similar medical condition.

Findings: Findings:  The physician reviewer found that Nature of Statutory Criteria/Case Summary: A patient has requested authorization and coverage for Ozempic solution pen-injector purchased and/or requested Ozempic solution pen-injector. The current obesity management guidelines put forward by the American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists (AACE) emphasize the fact that excessive adiposity can become dysfunctional and predispose patients to the development of many medical complications, including diabetes mellitus and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, among other chronic conditions. Pharmacotherapy for weight loss can be used for persons with a body mass index (BMI) greater than or equal to 30 kg/m2 or a BMI greater than or equal to 27 kg/m2 with adiposity-related complications, in conjunction with medical nutrition therapy, physical activity, and psychological interventions. Semaglutide has shown efficacy in the treatment of weight management and fatty liver disease. One study showed that the use of semaglutide in the form of Wegovy, accompanied with lifestyle modifications, led to an average 14.9% weight loss over 68 weeks, compared to a 2.4% weight loss in a placebo group that used lifestyle modifications only. Furthermore, a meta-analysis showed that treatment with semaglutide resulted in greater histological resolution of non-alcoholic steatohepatitis without worsening of liver fibrosis, in mostly obese or overweight patients. With that being noted, this patient’s most recent documented BMI is less than 27 kg/m2, and the records provided do not demonstrate any active weight loss efforts or failure of previous weight loss efforts. Thus, the results of the above studies do not support the use of Ozempic for this patient, considering the inclusion criteria for the referenced trials and the fact that semaglutide in the form of Wegovy was used rather than the requested Ozempic. Ozempic is a brand name of semaglutide that is approved only for the treatment of patients with diabetes. The use of medications approved for diabetes in managing obesity and overweight issues has led to a significant shortage of essential and lifesaving drugs for patients with diabetes who need the medication the most. Overall, the previous and/or future use of Ozempic was not and is not indicated for the treatment of this patient’s overweight state or presumed fatty liver disease. Given these findings, the Ozempic solution pen-injector purchased was not medically necessary for the treatment of the patient. Furthermore, the requested Ozempic solution pen-injector is not medically necessary for the treatment of the patient.Final Result: The reviewer determined that the medication at issue was not and is not medically necessary for the treatment of the patient’s medical condition. Therefore, the Health Plan’s denial should be upheld.Credentials/Qualifications: The reviewer is board certified in endocrinology, diabetes, and metabolism and is actively practicing. The reviewer is an expert in the treatment of the patient’s medical condition and knowledgeable about the proposed treatment through recent or current actual clinical experience treating those with the same or a similar medical condition.

The patient has requested treatment with a total hysterectomy and excision of endometriosis. Based on the available documentation, there is a lack of indication for these requested procedures. Specifically, laparoscopy was not performed, nor was a trial of a gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist (GnRH) or antagonist to see if it would control the pain. In addition, the patient has diagnoses of anxiety, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), anxiety, obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and post-concussive syndrome, and these could be affecting her pain sensation. Overall, it is unlikely that a hysterectomy will help manage the patient’s pain. The FDA has approved ISS as an indication for daily somatotropin therapy. Failures on at least two standard provocative tests compose one criterion to suggest pediatric growth hormone (GH) deficiency. This patient’s height z-score has remained well above -2.25 SDs over at least the past two years, prior to GH exposure. The patient’s normal stature with a height z-score above -2.0, predicted adult height within the normal range, and consistent with the familial pattern of growth, and lack of demonstrated endocrine pathology are consistent with normal variants of growth and development. Based on the available records, the requested Omnitrope is not medically necessary for this patient because she displays normal variant growth and development consistent with her familial pattern and the general population. She does not meet generally accepted clinical criteria for any FDA-approved indication for somatotropin or bioequivalent depot formulations such as Omnitrope. Findings: Findings:  The physician reviewer found that Nature of Statutory Criteria/Case Summary: The patient requested authorization and coverage for an Ozempic pen.Obesity is a disease associated with a significant increase in mortality and many health risks, including type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and coronary heart disease. According to the American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists (AACE) guidelines, patients who are overweight or with obesity and dyslipidemia should be considered for treatment with a weight-loss medication combined with lifestyle therapy when necessary to achieve sufficient improvements in lipids. Furthermore, these guidelines also state, “For patients who struggle with weight loss and who would receive health benefit from weight loss, management of medications that are contributing to weight gain and use of approved medications for chronic weight management along with lifestyle changes are appropriate.” Medications for weight loss are generally considered an option for a BMI greater than 27 kg/m2 with comorbidities such as diabetes or sleep apnea, or a BMI greater than 30 kg/m2 without comorbidities. The FDA approves medications for the treatment of obesity including orlistat, naltrexone/bupropion, phentermine, phentermine/topiramate, liraglutide, and semaglutide. Semaglutide for weight loss is branded as Ozempic, Wegovy, or Rybelsus. Semaglutide has demonstrated impressive efficacy in weight reduction. Both Ozempic and Wegovy are administered as a once-weekly subcutaneous injection, whereas Rybelsus is administered as a once-daily oral tablet. The FDA-approved dose titration schedules and available dosages are different for Ozempic and Wegovy, for example, while the starting dose is 0.25 mg weekly for both medications, Ozempic may be tolerated up as needed for effectiveness, whereas Wegovy has a forced titration schedule, and the approved therapeutic dose is 2.4 mg weekly. The FDA approves the use of Ozempic and Rybelsus for the treatment of type 2 diabetes and approves the use of Wegovy for the treatment of obesity. In this case, the patient has a BMI of 36.74 kg/m2 consistent with class 2 obesity. The guidelines support the use of FDA-approved medications for weight loss as an adjunct to lifestyle changes. The patient has demonstrated a long-term commitment to lifestyle changes. However, Ozempic is currently FDA-approved for type 2 diabetes and not for weight loss. Therefore, the requested medication, Ozempic pen, is not medically necessary for the treatment of the patient.Final Result: The reviewer determined that the requested services are not medically necessary for the treatment of the patient’s medical condition. Therefore, the Health Plan’s denial should be upheld.Credentials/Qualifications: The reviewer is board-certified in endocrinology, diabetes, and metabolism and is actively practicing.
